headline: Ukrainian Drone Hits Omsk at 2,500 km
deck: A cheap long-range airframe crossed defended airspace to strike Russia's largest refinery, placing western Siberia inside Ukraine's threat envelope. Kyiv says the gasoline-refinery campaign now covers all eleven major plants—and eight shadow-fleet tankers the same night.

art.caption: No rear area: western Siberia joins the threat envelope, eleven refineries out of eleven

body: Ukrainian drones struck the Gazprom Neft refinery on Omsk's northern outskirts overnight into 6 July, in one of the war's deepest attacks into Russian territory [E1][E5]. The facility lies more than 2,500 kilometres behind the border, a distance Kyiv's Special Operations Forces estimated at roughly 2,500 to 3,000 kilometres for the crossing airframe [E2][E4]. Western Siberia had sat beyond the envelope of earlier campaigns; this strike marks where that assumption breaks. Independent and Ukrainian reporting alike described the hit against Russia's largest oil refinery [E1][E3][E5].

The Omsk plant processed about 23 million tonnes last year, roughly 460,000 barrels a day, making it Russia's largest refinery by throughput [E3]. Reporting identified damage to the primary oil-processing unit, a section in continuous operation since 1955 [E2]. Ukrainian and specialist outlets characterized the hit as the first successful strike on a facility that had until then escaped the refining campaign [E3]. The target profile is fixed industrial hardware at continental distance, not a forward depot within existing drone envelopes.

Ukraine's General Staff and Special Operations Forces confirmed the strike and supplied the flight-distance estimate [E4]. In Kyiv's framing, Omsk was the last of Russia's eleven largest gasoline-producing refineries yet unhit, completing a set the campaign had pursued across the war [E4]. That completeness claim is Ukraine's own milestone language, not an independent audit of every Russian fuel asset [E4]. Still, hitting the largest remaining name on that list carries symbolic and operational weight for both sides.

Cheap long-range airframes crossing two and a half thousand kilometres of defended airspace to hit a fixed target end the idea of a secure Russian rear area [E2][E4]. Western Siberia now sits inside the same threat envelope as Bryansk or border-adjacent sites that have absorbed hits for months. Every refinery Moscow owns becomes a candidate for air-defence coverage that cannot simultaneously guard front-line batteries, logistics hubs, or Ukrainian approaches elsewhere. The trade is spatial: depth buys reach, and reach forces dispersion.

The same night extended the campaign from refining to the tanker logistics beneath it. Kyiv said Ukrainian forces struck eight sanctioned shadow-fleet tankers supplying Crimea [E6]. That parallel line of attack targets the vessels moving fuel when coastal plants and pipelines face disruption. Refining and delivery now share a single operational night [E6].

The strike lands on a Russian fuel system already under strain. Black Sea resort areas have seen petrol rationing, with volunteer and Cossack groups helping maintain order at filling stations [E7]. Moscow has turned to gasoline imports from India while domestic supply wobbles [E7]. Loosened fuel standards and queue management on the coast sketch the home-front ledger this deep strike adds to [E7].

Iran's missiles in the Strait of Hormuz the same period reminded global oil markets that both ends of the supply chain now face active shooters. Damage extent at Omsk remains contested as always: fires and a processing-unit hit appear in wire and open-source reporting [E2][E5]. Russian authorities have not published an assessment of the strike or its operational impact [E1][E5]. Without Moscow's accounting, no documented outage duration or throughput loss can be stated.
